title: Data Update 6 for 2026: In Search of Profitability!
gist: Damodaran uses his annual profitability data update to re-defend Friedman's profit-maximization mandate against ESG/stakeholder/sustainability framing, then shows most companies globally fail to earn their cost of capital, and argues AI will compress margins broadly (a 'slow-motion car wreck') rather than lift them, hitting today's highest-margin software firms hardest.

insights.claim: Milton Friedman's 1970 thesis that a business's social responsibility is to deliver and increase profits remains correct, according to Damodaran, and the decades of critique built on top of it (stakeholder wealth maximization, ESG, sustainability) have failed rather than improved on it.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should discount corporate ESG/sustainability messaging as a value driver and instead judge management on capital discipline and profitability.
insights.locator: The Business End Game

insights.claim: Damodaran labels stakeholder wealth maximization, ESG, and sustainability the 'theocratic trifecta' and argues all three failed for the same three structural reasons: they were rooted in virtue rather than business logic, their core terms were left deliberately amorphous, and they sold the false promise that companies and investors could 'do good' without any financial sacrifice.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A reusable lens for spotting the next values-branded investment fad: check whether it demands trade-offs be acknowledged or denies them entirely.
insights.quote: my categorization of people in these spaces as either useful idiots or feckless knaves stems from a refusal to face up to the inherent trade offs
insights.locator: theocratic trifecta

insights.claim: The apparent outperformance ('alpha') attributed to ESG investing in its early years was not caused by ESG screening itself but was accidental, driven almost entirely by ESG portfolios' overweight position in technology stocks during a period when tech rallied.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: ESG fund outperformance track records should be attributed to sector tilt, not screening methodology, when evaluating manager skill.
insights.locator: In 2020, when I first looked at ESG

insights.claim: Even on the one dimension where a rational case for ESG exists — risk reduction — Damodaran found no discernible effect on companies' cost of capital, with only anecdotal, mostly after-the-fact evidence that ESG protected firms from reputational or catastrophic risk.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Undercuts the pitch that ESG scores should be priced into cost-of-capital or credit models.
insights.locator: Even on risk

insights.claim: ESG has fallen out of favor over the last five years and been rebranded as 'sustainability' by the same industry ecosystem, but Damodaran views this as cosmetic repackaging rather than a substantive fix ('painting lipstick on a pig').
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Expect the next relabeling cycle to carry the same measurement and incentive problems as ESG did; don't pay a premium for the new label.
insights.quote: painting lipstick on a pig and then debating what shade of lipstick suits the pig best
insights.locator: the gravy train that lived off its largesse

insights.claim: Damodaran argues disclosure mandates, now a common regulatory fallback for improving corporate behavior, are unlikely to accomplish much and could do more harm than good, and that laws/regulations more broadly are blunt instruments that lose effectiveness as businesses can relocate across borders.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Skepticism warranted toward regulatory disclosure regimes (e.g., climate/ESG disclosure rules) as tools that meaningfully change corporate behavior or investor outcomes.
insights.locator: the disclosure route

themes.theme: 2025 Profitability and Margin Data by Sector

insights.claim: In 2025, global firms in Damodaran's sample generated $6.2 trillion in net income and $7.7 trillion in operating income on $72.4 trillion of revenue; US firms alone generated $2.2 trillion in net income and $2.9 trillion in operating income on $22.7 trillion of revenue.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Baseline aggregate profitability figures for benchmarking sector or company margins against the global/US corporate universe.
insights.locator: In the aggregate, global firms generated $6.2 trillion

insights.claim: Industrials carry the most revenue weight in the global sample, while health care generates the most revenue within the US subsample.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sector revenue weighting shapes how much any single sector's margin trend moves the aggregate number.
insights.locator: Across sectors, and looking at revenues

insights.claim: Technology has the highest weighted operating margin of any sector at 24.7%, but the median technology company's operating margin is only 3.41% globally (and -0.30% in the US), showing the sector is extremely top-heavy — a handful of mega-cap tech firms are highly profitable while most smaller tech companies are money-losing.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sector-level margin averages for tech are misleading for stock-picking; screen on company-level profitability, not sector aggregates, when evaluating smaller tech names.
insights.quote: the bigger tech companies are money machines in a sector that still contains a lot of younger and smaller money-losing firms
insights.locator: In technology, which has the highest weighted operating margin

insights.claim: At the industry level, basic chemicals has one of the lowest gross margins (9.31% aggregated/median), while system and application software has one of the highest, with a 71.72% gross margin, 33.21% operating margin, and 25.49% net margin — illustrating how wide the gap in unit economics is across industries.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Software's structurally superior unit economics is exactly what makes it the highest-value target for AI-driven margin compression discussed later in the piece.
insights.locator: At one end of the spectrum

insights.claim: Tobacco and railroads also rank among the highest-margin US industry groups alongside software, a result Damodaran flags as counter to intuition.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: High and stable margins can persist in mature, low-growth, regulation- or habit-protected industries, not just in tech.
insights.locator: in addition to tobacco and railroads

insights.claim: Margins are not computed for financial service firms in this dataset because their revenue figures are often unreported or economically meaningless, and gross/operating profit don't carry the same analytical value as for non-financial firms.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Analysts should avoid applying standard gross/operating margin screens to banks and financials; use returns-based metrics instead.
insights.locator: Note that the margins are not computed for financial service firms

themes.theme: Why margins have risen for two decades

insights.claim: S&P 500 net profit margins have trended clearly upward from 2000 to 2025.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A multi-decade margin uptrend is the backdrop against which any mean-reversion or margin-compression call (including the AI thesis) must be judged.
insights.locator: In the graph below, I look at the net profit margins

insights.claim: Damodaran rejects the 'accounting sleight-of-hand' explanation for rising margins, since recent accounting rule changes (expensing stock-based compensation and R&D) should have pushed reported earnings down, not up, and accountants have more power to shift income between periods than to permanently inflate its level.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Rules out a common bearish narrative (margins are an accounting illusion) as the primary driver of the multi-decade margin uptrend.
insights.locator: The most cynical explanation

insights.claim: The low-interest-rate era of the last decade is only a partial explanation for rising margins, and Damodaran is now skeptical of it as rates have normalized while margins have not reversed and the economy 'limps along.'
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: If margins were purely a low-rate phenomenon they should be mean-reverting now; their persistence argues for a structural rather than cyclical driver.
insights.locator: A second explanation is that the macroeconomic environment

insights.claim: The 'capital gained bargaining power over labor' explanation for rising margins (popular on the political left) doesn't hold broadly: margins in manufacturing and services — the highest employee-count, highest wage-cost sectors — have stagnated or fallen over the last 20 years, meaning neither capital nor labor gained in those sectors.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Complicates the labor-share-of-income narrative used to justify antitrust or labor-policy interventions targeting 'corporate power.'
insights.locator: A third explanation

insights.claim: The most salient driver of the 20-year margin uptrend, in Damodaran's view, is compositional: technology companies with structurally superior unit economics and economies of scale have displaced old-economy companies in the market, mechanically lifting aggregate market margins.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Index-level margin/valuation comparisons across decades are distorted by sector-composition shift; adjust for this before calling the market 'overvalued' on margin grounds.
insights.locator: The fourth, and in my view the most salient rationale

themes.theme: Value creation: most firms fail to earn their cost of capital

insights.claim: Profitability alone is too low a bar for business success; the real test is whether a company's return on equity/capital exceeds its cost of equity/capital, since invested capital always has an opportunity cost elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Screen for excess returns (ROE minus cost of equity, ROIC minus cost of capital), not just positive earnings, when judging whether a company is actually creating value.
insights.locator: Value Creation in Business

insights.claim: The width (strength) and depth (durability) of a company's competitive moat is what determines whether it can sustainably earn returns above its cost of equity/capital — a framework Damodaran credits partly to Michael Mauboussin's October 2024 Morgan Stanley piece.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Use moat width/depth as the qualitative overlay for explaining which companies will sustain excess returns rather than mean-revert.
insights.locator: width (strength of competitive advantages) and depth

insights.claim: Only 29% of global firms earn a return on equity above their cost of equity (28% for return on capital vs. cost of capital), and only 19%/17% respectively clear that bar by 5 percentage points or more — meaning the large majority of companies worldwide are not creating shareholder value on an accounting-returns basis.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Base rates matter: most publicly tracked companies globally destroy or merely tread water on value creation, which should temper broad-market optimism and reward selectivity.
insights.locator: only 29% (28%) of global firms earn returns

insights.claim: US companies collectively delivered excess returns of 8.44% on equity and 1.81% on capital in aggregate, with technology, consumer goods, and communication services the only US sectors posting double-digit excess returns at the aggregate level — evidence that large-cap firms in these sectors carry the market's value creation.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Value creation in the US market is narrowly concentrated in mega-cap tech/consumer/telecom names, reinforcing a barbell rather than broad-based bull case.
insights.locator: US companies collectively delivering excess returns

insights.claim: No country in Damodaran's regional breakdown has more than 50% of its firms earning above their cost of capital; Japan has the highest share of firms clearing the bar, while Canada and Australia have the lowest.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Country-level value-creation base rates argue against assuming any single developed market is broadly 'efficient' at earning above cost of capital; Japan screens relatively better on this metric.
insights.locator: Japan ranking highest in percentages

insights.claim: Using a global sample of 48,156 firms and a US sample of 5,994 firms, Damodaran finds that the share of companies beating their cost of capital rises as companies age through the corporate life cycle, but aggregated excess returns actually peak in middle age rather than continuing to rise — a pattern more pronounced in the US than globally.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't assume excess returns compound indefinitely with company maturity; the life-cycle framework implies mid-life is often the peak value-creation window, with decline following.
insights.locator: I break companies down by age into deciles

themes.theme: AI as a margin disruptor, not a margin booster

insights.claim: Damodaran takes a 'jaundiced view' of AI's effect on company profitability, predicting it will act as a disruptor that puts downward pressure on margins at most firms while increasing the advantage that already-larger firms hold within each business.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Positions AI as a net negative for average-firm margins and a further concentrator of winner-take-all dynamics — a reason to prefer scale leaders over the broad basket in AI-exposed industries.
insights.locator: A Profitability Wrap Up

insights.claim: Damodaran's causal argument against the 'AI makes companies more profitable' narrative: unless AI tools are exclusive to a given company (competitors can't buy the same or equivalent tools), the cost savings will be available industry-wide, triggering price competition that erodes the profitability gains rather than letting them flow to the bottom line.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Be skeptical of corporate 'AI will boost our margins' guidance unless the company has a genuinely proprietary AI advantage; broadly available AI tools get competed away via pricing.
insights.locator: Unless the AI tools that you are talking about are exclusive

insights.claim: Damodaran draws a direct historical analogy: online retail was initially seen as a boon by brick-and-mortar retailers but ended up destroying most of them and reducing margins for the retail sector collectively over the past three decades — offered as the template for how AI will play out across industries.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A historical base-rate reference point: treat 'this new tech will help incumbents' claims with skepticism when the tech is broadly accessible, since retail's incumbents were similarly wrong about e-commerce.
insights.locator: a revisit of the US retail sector over the last three decades

insights.claim: The recent AI-driven sell-off in software stocks reflects investors preemptively pricing in the risk that AI will put downward pressure on software margins, even though the actual revenue/margin decline hasn't shown up in the numbers yet — which Damodaran argues is exactly what efficient markets are supposed to do, since these same stocks were earlier marked up ahead of realized margin gains.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Frames the software sell-off as rational forward pricing of margin risk rather than an overreaction, arguing against 'buy the dip' framing based on trailing fundamentals alone.
insights.quote: You live by the sword (expectations of growth and high margins), you die by it (expectations that growth rates will hit a cliff and margins will decline)!
insights.locator: the AI sell off that we have seen play out

insights.claim: Damodaran predicts AI's effect on overall corporate profitability will be a 'slow-motion car wreck' — gradual margin slippage playing out over time rather than a sudden shock, but ultimately damaging.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sets a multi-year rather than a quarterly time horizon for watching AI-driven margin compression play out across sectors.
insights.quote: I do think that the AI disruption will be more akin to a slow-motion car wreck
insights.locator: Time will tell

insights.claim: Damodaran argues consumers will be net beneficiaries of AI-driven cost and price competition, but investors and employees in disrupted companies will bear a cost that, for a sizable number of them, outweighs those consumer benefits.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Implies a distributional trade: broad consumer-welfare gains from AI may come paired with concentrated losses for shareholders/workers in disrupted incumbents — relevant for sector allocation, not just index-level calls.
insights.locator: As consumers, we will benefit

themes.theme: Crumbling moats and the winner-take-all tilt

insights.claim: Over the last four decades, competitive moats have generally crumbled due to global competition and disruption, yet the corporate landscape has simultaneously tilted toward larger firms as more businesses become winner(s)-take-all — the context in which Damodaran situates his skeptical view of AI's effect on profitability.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Reconciles seemingly contradictory trends (weaker moats overall, but more concentrated winners) — favor scale/incumbency within disrupted industries rather than assuming disruption is broadly democratizing.
insights.locator: moats have crumbled
